original_text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, but the leave is unpaid with only job protection guaranteed.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, providing a small flat weekly stipend of $150 regardless of prior earnings.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, replacing 40% of their regular wages up to a modest cap.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, replacing 80% of their regular wages up to a generous cap.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, replacing 100% of their regular wages with no income cap.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A new U.S. federal law introduces universal paid parental leave of 12 weeks for all workers, replacing 100% of their regular wages with no income cap and providing an additional monthly newborn-care stipend.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the generosity of the pay replacement: from completely unpaid (tier 1), to a minimal flat stipend (tier 2), to 40% partial replacement (tier 3), to unspecified moderate pay (tier 4), to 80% replacement (tier 5), to full 100% replacement with no cap (tier 6), to full replacement plus an additional stipend (tier 7). The core scenario—a federal 12-week universal parental leave law—remains constant throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because higher pay replacement rates reduce the financial burden on new parents more effectively, and adding a supplemental care stipend on top of full wage replacement represents the most generous possible version of the policy.
